Watch — 2-minute explainer

Whole exome sequencing, explained.

A short overview of what WES looks at and why it matters for the children you serve — useful to watch yourself or share with a family.

Why genetic testing matters here

An autism diagnosis describes behavior. WES can explain the biology behind it.

For many children, behavioral and developmental presentations have an identifiable genetic basis. Finding it can change medical management, surveillance, eligibility for services, and family planning — without changing the behavioral support you already provide.

~20,000
genes evaluated in a single test
25–40%
diagnostic yield in the ASD / DD / ID population
~85%
of disease-causing variants live in the exome
1 swab
mouthwash rinse or buccal swab — no blood draw

What a WES result can do for a family

  • Confirm or establish an underlying genetic diagnosis
  • Guide medical management and specialty referrals
  • Inform surveillance for associated health conditions
  • Reduce the need for further open-ended testing
  • Provide recurrence-risk information for family planning
  • Support eligibility documentation for services and resources

How GeneID supports the result

  • Insurance verified before any testing begins
  • Full medical-necessity documentation submitted on the family's behalf
  • Board-certified lab director sign-out (CLIA / CAP)
  • Genetic counseling pathway available for positive findings
  • Results returned to the ordering provider with clear interpretation
Who to refer

The patients on your caseload are often the ideal candidates.

Xenome WES is most informative for children whose presentation suggests a possible Mendelian or genetic basis. If a child meets any of the criteria below, they may be a strong referral.

Strong referral indicators

  • Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)
  • Developmental delay or intellectual disability
  • Epilepsy or seizures of unknown cause
  • Multiple congenital anomalies or dysmorphic features
  • Undiagnosed after prior non-diagnostic testing
  • Family history of a Mendelian / inherited disorder

What you'll need to refer

  • Patient and parent/guardian contact information
  • Referring practice and clinician name, if known
  • Brief clinical reason for referral
  • Ordering provider, if known (GeneID can assist)
  • That's it — GeneID coordinates the rest with the family
How a referral works

Three steps. The family is fully supported at every one.

1

You refer the family

Submit the secure referral form below. It takes about two minutes and goes straight to the GeneID team.

2

GeneID handles everything

We reach out to the family, verify insurance, coordinate the ordering provider, and arrange the simple oral-sample collection — no blood draw.

3

Results inform care

The WES report goes to the ordering provider with clear interpretation, and a genetic-counseling pathway is available for any positive finding.

Cost should never be the barrier. Xenome WES is billed to the patient's health plan. GeneID verifies coverage and submits full medical-necessity documentation before testing — roughly 98% of patients pay $0, and families are never surprised by a bill.
A complement, not a competitor

Genetic answers strengthen the work you already do.

A WES diagnosis doesn't replace behavioral therapy — it adds context. Knowing the underlying genetics can help the family's medical team manage co-occurring conditions, anticipate needs, and access additional resources, all while the child continues the ABA program you provide.

You stay the family's behavioral expert. GeneID simply opens a door to answers many families have been searching for.

What stays with you

  • The therapeutic relationship with the family
  • Day-to-day behavioral assessment and intervention
  • Program goals and clinical direction

What GeneID takes on

  • Insurance verification and medical-necessity documentation
  • Provider coordination and sample logistics
  • Reporting and the genetic-counseling pathway
